classes.name: nam.models.metadata.UserMetadata
documentation:
Metadata that users provide for a NAM model

```
param name

A "human-readable" name for the model.

param modeled_by

Who made the model

param gear_type

Type of gear.

param gear_make

Make of the gear.

param gear_model

Model of the gear.

param tone_type

What sort of tone the gear has.

input_level_dbu

What analog loudness, in dBu, corresponds to 0 dbFS input to the
model.

output_level_dbu

What analog loudness, in dBu, corresponds to 0 dbFS outputted by
the model.
```

methods.name: nam.models.recurrent.LSTM.__init__
documentation:
---
param hidden_size: for LSTM
param input_size: |-
  Usually 1 (mono input). A catnet extending this might change
  it and provide the parametric inputs as additional input dimensions.
param train_burn_in: |-
  Detach calculations from first (this many) samples when
  training to burn in the hidden state.
param train_truncate: |-
  detach the hidden & cell states every this many steps
  during training so that backpropagation through time is faster + to simulate
  better starting states for h(t0)&c(t0) (instead of zeros)
  TODO recognition head to start the hidden state in a good place?
---

* Head weights
* Head bias

classes.name: nam.models.conv_net._Functional
documentation: Define a layer by a function w/ no params

classes.name: nam.models.conv_net.ConvNet

methods.name: nam.models.conv_net.ConvNet._export_weights
documentation:
weights are serialized to weights.npy in the following order:
* (expand: no params)
* loop blocks 0,...,L-1
    * conv:
        * weight (Cout, Cin, K)
        * bias (if no batchnorm) (Cout)
    * BN
        * running mean
        * running_var
        * weight (Cout)
        * bias (Cout)
        * eps ()
* head
    * weight (C, 1, 1)
    * bias (1, 1)
* (flatten: no params)

A straightforward convolutional neural network.

Works surprisingly well!

methods.name: nam.models.wavenet._Layer.forward
documentation:
---
param h: (B,DX,L2) Conditioning. If first, ignored.
param x: (B,C,L1) From last layer
return: |-
  If not final:

  : (B,C,L1-d) to next layer
    (B,C,L1-d) to mixer

  If final, next layer is None
---

classes.name: nam.models.wavenet._Layers

methods.name: nam.models.wavenet._Layers.forward
documentation:
---
param c: (B,Dc,L) condition
param x: (B,Dx,L) layer input
return: |-
  (B,Dc,L-R+1) head input
  (B,Dc,L-R+1) layer output
---

Takes in the input and condition (and maybe the head input so far); outputs the
layer output and head input.

The original WaveNet only uses one of these, but you can stack multiple of this
module to vary the channels throughout with minimal extra channel-changing conv
layers.

classes.name: nam.train.metadata.LatencyCalibrationWarnings
documentation:
Things that aren't necessarily wrong with the latency calibration but are
worth looking into.

```
param matches_lookahead

The calibrated latency is as far forard as
possible, i.e. the very first sample we looked at tripped the trigger.
That's probably not a coincidence but the trigger is too sensitive.

param disagreement_too_high

The range of the latency estimates is greater
than the max_disagreement_threshold. Indication that something may have
gone wrong.
```

---
param filename: Where to load from

param preroll: Drop this many samples off the front
param rate: Expected sample rate. `None` allows for anything.
param require_match: |-
  If not `None`, assert that the data you get matches the shape
  and other characteristics of another audio file at the provided location
param required_shape: |-
  If not `None`, assert that the audio loaded is of shape
  `(num_samples, num_channels)`.
param required_wavinfo: |-
  If not `None`, assert that the WAV info of the laoded audio
  matches that provided.
---

documentation: NOTE: This breaks the gradient tape!

functions.name: nam.data.register_dataset_initializer
documentation:
If you have other data set types, you can register their initializer by name using
this.

For example, the basic NAM is registered by default under the name "default", but if
it weren't, you could register it like this:

```pycon
>>> from nam import data
>>> data.register_dataset_initializer("parametric", MyParametricDataset.init_from_config)
```

```
param name

The name that'll be used in the config to ask for the data set type

param constructor

The constructor that'll be fed the config.
```

classes.name: nam.data.StartStopError

classes.name: nam.data.Dataset

methods.name: nam.data.Dataset.__init__
documentation:
---
param delay: |-
  In samples. Positive means we get rid of the start of x, end of y
  (i.e. we are correcting for an alignment error in which y is delayed behind
  x). If a non-integer delay is provided, then y is interpolated, with
  the extra sample removed.
param input_gain: |-
  In dB. If the input signal wasn't fed to the amp at unity
  gain, you can indicate the gain here. The data set will multipy the raw
  audio file by the specified gain so that the true input signal amplitude
  experienced by the signal chain will be provided as input to the model. If
  you are using a reamping setup, you can estimate this by reamping a
  completely dry signal (i.e. connecting the interface output directly back
  into the input with which the guitar was originally recorded.)
param nx: |-
  The number of samples required as input for the model. For example,
  for a ConvNet, this would be the receptive field.
param ny: |-
  How many samples to provide as the output array for a single "datum".
  It's usually more computationally-efficient to provide a larger `ny` than 1
  so that the forward pass can process more audio all at once. However, this
  shouldn't be too large or else you won't be able to provide a large batch
  size (where each input-output pair could be something substantially
  different and improve batch diversity).
param require_input_pre_silence: |-
  If provided, require that this much time (in
  seconds) preceding the start of the data set (`start`) have a silent input.
  If it's not, then raise an exception because the output due to it will leak
  into the data set that we're trying to use. If `None`, don't assert.
param sample_rate: Sample rate for the data
param start: |-
  [DEPRECATED; use start_samples instead.] In samples; clip x and y
  at this point. Negative values are taken from the end of the audio.
param start_samples: |-
  Clip x and y at this point. Negative values are taken from
  the end of the audio.
param start_seconds: |-
  Clip x and y at this point. Negative values are taken from
  the end of the audio. Requires providing `sample_rate`.
param stop: |-
  Clip x and y at this point. Negative values are taken from the end
  of the audio.
param stop_seconds: |-
  Clip x and y at this point. Negative values are taken from
  the end of the audio. Requires providing `sample_rate`.
param x: The input signal. A 1D array.
param y: The associated output from the model. A 1D array.
param y_scale: |-
  Multiplies the output signal by a factor (e.g. if the data are
  too quiet).
---

methods.name: nam.data.Dataset.parse_config
documentation:
---
param config: |-
  Must contain:

  : x_path (path-like)
    y_path (path-like)

  May contain:

  : sample_rate (int)
    y_preroll (int)
    allow_unequal_lengths (bool)

  Must NOT contain:

  : x (torch.Tensor) - loaded from x_path
    y (torch.Tensor) - loaded from y_path

  Everything else is passed on to \_\_init\_\_
---

methods.name: nam.data.Dataset._validate_start_stop
documentation:
Parse the requested start and stop trim points.

These may be valid indices in Python, but probably point to invalid usage, so
we will raise an exception if something fishy is going on (e.g. starting after
the end of the file, etc)

```
return

parsed start/stop (if valid).
```

methods.name: nam.data.Dataset._validate_preceding_silence
documentation:
Make sure that the input is silent before the starting index.
If it's not, then the output from that non-silent input will leak into the data
set and couldn't be predicted!

This assumes that silence is indeed required. If it's not, then don't call this!

See: Issue #252

```
param x

Input

param start

Where the data starts

param silent_samples

How many are expected to be silent
```
Take a pair of matched audio files and serve input + output pairs.

functions.name: nam.train.colab.run
documentation:
---
param delay: |-
  How far the output algs the input due to round-trip latency during
  reamping, in samples.
param epochs: How many epochs we'll train for.
param ignore_checks: Ignores the data quality checks and YOLOs it
param lr: The initial learning rate
param lr_decay: The amount by which the learning rate decays each epoch
param seed: RNG seed for reproducibility.
param stage_1_channels: The number of channels in the WaveNet's first stage.
param stage_2_channels: The number of channels in the WaveNet's second stage.
param user_metadata: User-specified metadata to include in the .nam file.
---

modules.name: nam.train.lightning_module
documentation:
Implements the base PyTorch Lightning module.
This is meant to combine an actual model (subclassed from `..models.base.BaseNet`)
along with loss function boilerplate.

For the base *PyTorch* model containing the actual architecture, see `..models.base`.

classes.name: nam.train.lightning_module.ValidationLoss
documentation:
mse: mean squared error
esr: error signal ratio (Eq. (10) from
    https://www.mdpi.com/2076-3417/10/3/766/htm
    NOTE: Be careful when computing ESR on minibatches! The average ESR over
    a minibatch of data not the same as the ESR of all of the same data in
    the minibatch calculated over at once (because of the denominator).
    (Hint: think about what happens if one item in the minibatch is all
    zeroes...)

classes.name: nam.train.lightning_module.LossConfig

methods.name: nam.train.lightning_module.LossConfig.apply_mask
documentation:
---
param args: (L,) or (B,)
return: (L-M,) or (B, L-M)
---

---
param dc_weight: Weight for the DC loss term. If 0, ignored.
param mask_first: How many of the first samples to ignore when comptuing the loss.
param mrstft_weight: |-
  Multi-resolution short-time Fourier transform loss
  coefficient. None means to skip; 2e-4 works pretty well if one wants to use it.
param pre_emph_coef: |-
  Coefficient of 1st-order pre-emphasis filter from
  <https://www.mdpi.com/2076-3417/10/3/766>. Paper value: 0.95.
params val_loss: Which loss to track for the best model checkpoint.
---

\:param [pre]

  None means we don't know and it has to be calibrated.
param num_epochs: How many epochs to train for.

  Stop training if the ESR gets better than this. If None, don't
  stop.

May throw a tk.TclError indicating something went wrong getting the value.

modules.name: nam.train.core
documentation:
The core of the "simplified trainer"

Used by the GUI and Colab trainers.

functions.name: nam.train.core._detect_input_version
documentation:
Check to see if the input matches any of the known inputs

```
return

version, strong match
```

functions.name: nam.train.core._calibrate_latency_v_all
documentation:
Calibrate the delay in teh input-output pair based on blips.
This only uses the blips in the first set of blip locations!

```
param y

The output audio, in complete.
```

functions.name: nam.train.core._analyze_latency
documentation:
---
param is_proteus: Forget the version; d
---

functions.name: nam.train.core._check_data
documentation:
Ensure that everything should go smoothly

```
return

True if looks good
```

functions.name: nam.train.core.train
documentation:
---
param fast_dev_run: One-step training, used for tests.
param lr_decay: =1-gamma for Exponential learning rate decay.
param threshold_esr: Stop training if ESR is better than this. Ignore if `None`.
---

functions.name: nam.train.core._check_audio_lengths
documentation:
Check that the input and output have the right lengths compared to each
other.

```
param input_path

Path to input audio

param output_path

Path to output audio

param max_under_seconds

If not None, the maximum amount by which the
output can be shorter than the input. Should be non-negative i.e. a
value of 1.0 means that the output can't be more than a second shorter
than the input.

param max_over_seconds

If not None, the maximum amount by which the
output can be longer than the input. Should be non-negative i.e. a
value of 1.0 means that the output can't be more than a second longer
than the input.
```

functions.name: nam.train.core.validate_data
documentation:
Just do the checks to make sure that the data are ok.

* Version identification
* Latency calibration
* Other checks

variables.name: nam.train.core._DELAY_CALIBRATION_SAFETY_FACTOR
documentation: Might be able to make this zero...

classes.name: nam.train.core._ValidationStopping
documentation:
Callback to indicate to stop training if the validation metric is good enough,
without the other conditions that EarlyStopping usually forces like patience.

methods.name: nam.train.core._ModelCheckpoint._get_nam_filepath
documentation: Given a .ckpt filepath, figure out a .nam for it.
Extension to model checkpoint to save a .nam file as well as the .ckpt file.
